title: SQL Developer — Paper 3
description: Developer-focused SQL scenarios covering query writing, joins, filtering, aggregations, CTEs, indexes, transactions, window functions, stored procedures, and data safety.

questions.text: A developer writes a report query that joins five tables and selects 40 columns with SELECT *. The DBA notices the query causes excessive memory grant warnings. Which refactoring addresses this most directly?

options.text: Add a MAXDOP hint to limit parallelism
Select only the columns needed by the report, reducing the row width so sort and hash operations require less memory
Convert all INNER JOINs to LEFT JOINs
Add ORDER BY to every subquery in the joins

questions.text: A developer writes the following to count active users who have placed at least one order: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Users u JOIN Orders o ON u.UserId = o.UserId WHERE u.IsActive = 1. What is a potential overcounting issue?

options.text: COUNT(*) counts NULL rows which inflates the total
The JOIN multiplies user rows — a user with 5 orders is counted 5 times; COUNT(DISTINCT u.UserId) is needed
WHERE must appear before JOIN in standard SQL
COUNT(*) cannot be used with a JOIN

questions.text: A developer uses COALESCE in an ORDER BY clause: ORDER BY COALESCE(PreferredName, FirstName). What does this accomplish and when is it useful?

options.text: COALESCE cannot be used in ORDER BY; a CASE expression must be used instead
It sorts by PreferredName when it is not NULL, falling back to FirstName for rows where PreferredName is NULL, ensuring all rows sort by the most relevant available name
It sorts NULL values to the top of the result set regardless of column value
COALESCE in ORDER BY causes a full table scan because it wraps the column in a function

questions.text: A developer wants to use NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY Revenue DESC) to bucket customers into quartiles. The result set has 101 rows. How will NTILE distribute the extra row?

options.text: The extra row is discarded
NTILE raises an error when the row count is not evenly divisible
NTILE distributes the remainder rows one each to the first buckets — bucket 1 gets 26 rows, buckets 2-4 get 25 rows each
All four buckets receive 25 rows and the extra row is assigned to a fifth bucket

questions.text: A developer uses sp_executesql with a parameterised query: EXEC sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE CustomerId = @cid', N'@cid INT', @cid = @InputId. How does this protect against SQL injection compared to string concatenation?

options.text: sp_executesql encrypts the query before execution
The parameter @cid is passed separately from the query string; user input is never interpreted as SQL syntax, so malicious strings cannot modify query structure
sp_executesql validates that @InputId is a numeric value before execution
sp_executesql runs in a sandboxed context with no table access

questions.text: A query on a Logs table with 50M rows uses: WHERE CONVERT(DATE, LogTimestamp) = '2025-03-15'. An index exists on LogTimestamp (DATETIME). Why does this query not use the index efficiently?

options.text: DATE is not compatible with DATETIME indexes
Wrapping LogTimestamp in CONVERT() makes the expression non-sargable — the optimiser cannot seek into the index because each row must be converted at scan time
The index on DATETIME columns is never used for equality filters
CONVERT requires a CAST hint to enable index seeks

questions.text: A developer handles an order placement transaction. The code inserts an order header, then inserts order lines. A constraint violation occurs on the second order line insert. The developer catches the error but does NOT explicitly ROLLBACK. What state is the transaction in?

options.text: The entire transaction is automatically rolled back by SQL Server
The transaction remains open and uncommitted; subsequent commits would commit the partial data unless a ROLLBACK is issued
Only the failed statement is undone; all prior inserts are committed
The connection is automatically closed after a constraint violation in a transaction

questions.text: A developer writes: SELECT Department, SUM(Salary) AS TotalPay, COUNT(DISTINCT EmployeeId) AS HeadCount FROM Employees GROUP BY Department. Which statement about COUNT(DISTINCT EmployeeId) is true?

options.text: COUNT(DISTINCT col) is invalid inside GROUP BY queries
COUNT(DISTINCT EmployeeId) counts unique EmployeeId values within each department group, useful when joins might introduce duplicate rows
COUNT(DISTINCT col) is identical to COUNT(col) when the column has no duplicates in the raw table
DISTINCT inside COUNT() is only supported in SQL Server 2019 and later

questions.text: A developer writes a CTE to find the second-highest salary in a company. They write: WITH Ranked AS (SELECT Salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS Rnk FROM Employees) SELECT Salary FROM Ranked WHERE Rnk = 2. Why is DENSE_RANK preferred over RANK for this use case?

options.text: DENSE_RANK is faster than RANK on large tables
If multiple employees share the highest salary, RANK would skip rank 2 entirely; DENSE_RANK ensures there is always a rank 2 value representing the second distinct salary level
RANK cannot be used inside CTEs
DENSE_RANK supports NULL salaries while RANK does not

questions.text: A developer sees that SQL Server's Missing Index DMV suggests an index on (EmailAddress) INCLUDE (FirstName, LastName, CreatedDate) for a Users table. What does the INCLUDE clause mean and why is it suggested?

options.text: INCLUDE columns become part of the index key and are used for filtering
INCLUDE columns are stored at the leaf level of the index but not in the key; they allow the query to be satisfied entirely from the index without a Key Lookup back to the clustered index
INCLUDE prevents the index from being used for sorting operations
INCLUDE columns are only stored in memory and are not persisted to disk

questions.text: A developer uses ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY CustomerId ORDER BY OrderDate DESC) AS RowNum and filters WHERE RowNum = 1. What does this return?

options.text: The first order ever placed by each customer
The most recent order per customer, with exactly one row returned per customer
All orders where the customer placed exactly one order total
The order with the lowest OrderDate per customer

questions.text: A developer issues an UPDATE without a WHERE clause on a 200,000 row Prices table in a production database. After the statement executes, they realise the mistake. What is the correct immediate action assuming the session has not been closed?

options.text: Run DELETE to remove the incorrectly updated rows
If the UPDATE was inside an explicit transaction that has not been committed, issue ROLLBACK TRANSACTION immediately
Restore the entire database from backup before doing anything else
Run UPDATE again with the original values from memory

questions.text: A developer queries Employees with a LEFT JOIN to Departments: SELECT e.Name, d.DeptName FROM Employees e LEFT JOIN Departments d ON e.DeptId = d.DeptId. Three employees have a DeptId that does not exist in Departments. What appears in the DeptName column for those rows?

options.text: An empty string ''
NULL
The word 'UNKNOWN'
Those rows are excluded from the result

questions.text: A developer is querying two tables: SalesQ1 and SalesQ2 with identical column structures and wants to combine results including duplicates across both quarters. Which set operation should be used?

options.text: UNION, which removes duplicates
UNION ALL, which retains all rows including duplicates across both result sets
INTERSECT, which returns rows common to both
EXCEPT, which returns rows in Q1 not in Q2

questions.text: An e-commerce application uses SNAPSHOT isolation to allow readers and writers to work concurrently. A developer notices that a long-running transaction is causing tempdb to grow rapidly. What is the cause?

options.text: SNAPSHOT isolation locks all rows in tempdb
SNAPSHOT isolation stores row versions in tempdb; the longer a transaction runs, the more versions accumulate as other transactions modify the data, growing tempdb
tempdb growth is unrelated to isolation level
SNAPSHOT isolation disables auto-shrink on tempdb

questions.text: A developer writes: SELECT ProductId, CASE WHEN Stock > 100 THEN 'High' WHEN Stock > 10 THEN 'Medium' ELSE 'Low' END AS StockLevel FROM Products WHERE StockLevel = 'High'. What is wrong?

options.text: CASE WHEN cannot be used in SELECT alongside WHERE
The alias StockLevel cannot be referenced in the WHERE clause of the same query level; the CASE expression must be repeated in WHERE or the query wrapped in a subquery
ELSE is not valid in a searched CASE expression
CASE WHEN with a string result requires a CAST in the WHERE clause

questions.text: A developer stores a customer's date of birth as VARCHAR(10) in the format 'DD-MM-YYYY'. When they try to sort customers by age they get incorrect results. What is the root cause and solution?

options.text: VARCHAR columns cannot be sorted; a CAST is always required
Storing dates as VARCHAR means sorting is alphabetical (by day first) rather than chronological; dates should be stored as DATE or DATETIME2 data types for correct temporal ordering
The format 'DD-MM-YYYY' must be converted to 'YYYY-MM-DD' using REPLACE before sorting
DATE columns sort differently to DATETIME columns, causing discrepancies

questions.text: A developer adds TRY...CATCH to a stored procedure that runs multiple INSERTs. Inside the CATCH block they want to surface the original error to the caller. Which statement achieves this in SQL Server?

options.text: RETURN -1
THROW; (re-throws the original error with its original error number, severity, and state)
RAISERROR('An error occurred', 16, 1)
SELECT ERROR_MESSAGE()

questions.text: A developer writes: SELECT ProductId, SaleMonth, SUM(Revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY ProductId ORDER BY SaleMonth) AS RunningRevenue FROM MonthlySales. What does this query produce for each row?

options.text: The total revenue for the product across all months
A cumulative sum of Revenue from the first month up to the current month, reset for each product
The revenue for the current month only
The average monthly revenue per product

questions.text: A developer queries an Orders table frequently with: WHERE ShippedDate IS NULL AND OrderStatus = 'Processing'. Which composite index definition best supports this query?

options.text: CREATE INDEX IX_Orders ON Orders(ShippedDate)
CREATE INDEX IX_Orders ON Orders(OrderStatus, ShippedDate) WHERE ShippedDate IS NULL AND OrderStatus = 'Processing'
CREATE INDEX IX_Orders ON Orders(OrderId) INCLUDE (ShippedDate, OrderStatus)
CREATE CLUSTERED INDEX IX_Orders ON Orders(ShippedDate, OrderStatus)

questions.text: A developer writes a correlated subquery: SELECT c.CustomerId, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Orders o WHERE o.CustomerId = c.CustomerId) AS OrderCount FROM Customers c. On 200k customers, the query is very slow. What is the most efficient rewrite?

options.text: Replace the subquery with EXISTS
LEFT JOIN Customers to a pre-aggregated subquery: (SELECT CustomerId, COUNT(*) AS OrderCount FROM Orders GROUP BY CustomerId) and use COALESCE to handle NULLs for customers with no orders
Use CROSS APPLY instead of a correlated subquery
Add an index on Customers.CustomerId

questions.text: A developer must write a CASE expression that labels a product's margin: 'High' if margin > 30%, 'Medium' if 10-30%, and 'Low' otherwise. The margin is calculated as (SalePrice - CostPrice) / SalePrice. What risk exists when CostPrice equals SalePrice?

options.text: The CASE expression cannot handle negative results
If SalePrice is 0, the division produces a divide-by-zero error; this must be guarded with NULLIF(SalePrice, 0) or a CASE WHEN SalePrice = 0 THEN NULL ELSE ... END
CASE expressions automatically return 0 for division by zero
SQL Server converts 0/0 to NULL silently

questions.text: A developer normalises a customer address into a separate Addresses table linked by CustomerId. Later, a report queries customer name and address for 100k customers requiring a JOIN every time. A colleague suggests denormalising. What is the main trade-off?

options.text: Denormalisation increases the number of tables and JOINs required
Denormalisation improves read performance by eliminating the JOIN but risks data anomalies — updating a customer's address must be done in multiple places or inconsistencies arise
Normalisation always performs better than denormalisation for reads
Denormalisation violates SQL Server constraints and cannot be implemented

questions.text: A developer needs to find rows in a Products table where the Description column contains the word 'organic' regardless of case. Which approach is efficient on a case-insensitive collation database?

options.text: WHERE LOWER(Description) = 'organic'
WHERE Description LIKE '%organic%'
WHERE CHARINDEX('organic', Description) > 0
Both b and c achieve the same result on a case-insensitive collation; neither enables an index seek since the leading wildcard in LIKE prevents it

questions.text: A developer writes a query that returns product sales grouped by year. They use GROUP BY YEAR(SaleDate), ProductId. A colleague points out the query could be written with GROUP BY SaleYear, ProductId where SaleYear is a calculated column. What is the issue with using an alias in GROUP BY in SQL Server?

options.text: SQL Server allows column aliases in GROUP BY without restriction
SQL Server does not allow SELECT aliases to be used in GROUP BY; the expression YEAR(SaleDate) must be repeated in GROUP BY
Aliases can be used in GROUP BY only if defined in a subquery
Aliases in GROUP BY are supported only in SQL Server 2022

questions.text: A developer encounters a deadlock and the SQL Server deadlock graph shows two SPIDs waiting on each other. The DBA recommends adding a READPAST hint. What does READPAST do?

options.text: READPAST reads uncommitted data to avoid waiting for locks
READPAST skips locked rows rather than waiting for the lock to be released, which can reduce deadlocks but means some rows may be temporarily invisible to the reader
READPAST upgrades the query to SERIALIZABLE isolation
READPAST forces SQL Server to choose a different execution plan

questions.text: A developer uses FIRST_VALUE(Revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY ProductId ORDER BY SaleMonth) AS FirstMonthRevenue. What does this return for each row?

options.text: The revenue from the row immediately before the current row
The revenue from the first month in the ordered partition for the same product, repeated on every row for that product
The minimum revenue within the product partition
NULL for all rows except the first row of each partition

questions.text: A developer designs a table for a multi-tenant SaaS app where each tenant's data must be isolated. They add a TenantId column with a foreign key to a Tenants table and ensure all queries include WHERE TenantId = @TenantId. What additional database-level safety layer could enforce this at the row level automatically?

options.text: A CHECK constraint on TenantId > 0
Row-Level Security (RLS) using a security policy and predicate function that filters rows based on the current session's tenant context
A UNIQUE constraint on (TenantId, UserId)
Separate databases per tenant

questions.text: A developer uses INTERSECT to compare two sets of CustomerIds — one from an email campaign list and one from the purchases table. What does the result contain?

options.text: All CustomerIds from both lists combined with duplicates removed
CustomerIds that appear in both lists — customers who were on the email list AND made a purchase
CustomerIds that appear in the email list but not in the purchases table
CustomerIds unique to each list, excluding those appearing in both

questions.text: A developer writes a stored procedure that reads a large dataset and processes rows in a CURSOR loop, issuing an UPDATE per row. With 50,000 rows this takes 4 minutes. What is the primary reason and how should it be rewritten?

options.text: CURSORs are not supported inside stored procedures

A CURSOR issues one round-trip per row, accumulating transaction and lock overhead for each row; replacing the cursor with a single set-based UPDATE statement processes all rows in one operation and is typically orders of magnitude faster
The procedure should use BULK INSERT instead
The CURSOR should be changed from FAST_FORWARD to STATIC to improve speed
